text: In this video, we would like to present an intra-anal fistulotomy with marsupialization. We would like to start by reminding you of Park's classification of most common fistulas. And also the Goodsall's rule that states if the external opening is anterior to the line, the fistula usually runs directly into the anal canal. And if the external opening is posterior to the line, the fistula usually curves to the posterior midline of the canal. A fifty-year-old gentleman presented at our clinic complaining of constant secretion through anus. From his previous medical history he had intersphincteric fistula that was treated several times. MRI was performed. High intersphincteric fistula was detected. Internal opening at the level of dentate line posterior midline with no external opening. This is the scheme of fistula tract. According to Park's classification, the fistula in our case was intersphincteric fistula with rectal opening and without a perineal opening.

The patient is positioned in a prone jackknife position with buttocks taped aside. Retractors are placed to expose the surgical field. A probe is inserted through the internal opening to explore the high intersphincteric fistula tract, revealing several milliliters of purulent discharge. A swab is taken for microbiological analysis.

The surgeon performs a phthalotomy using monopolar electrocautery with a probe placed from the internal opening to the end of the blind tract. The open fistula tract is then curetted, followed by marsupialization.
A hemostatic sponge is inserted into the surgical site. The video shows the patient two months post-operation, with no visible signs of fecal incontinence.
Wexner's score before and after operation was zero. Thank you.
